title: Quantum charge pumping in helical systems: A comparative study of short- and long-range hopping
authors: Leila Eslami, Santanu K. Maiti, Fatemeh Bourbour
abstract: Using the Keldysh non-equilibrium Green's function approach, we investigate charge pumping through a single-stranded helical structure described by a tight-binding model that includes either short-range hopping (SRH) or long-range hopping (LRH). While quantum pumping has been studied in various low-dimensional systems, the detailed behavior of the spectral current and the pumped dc current in helical geometries in the presence of higher-order electron hopping (beyond nearest neighbors) has not yet been systematically explored. Here, we focus on the interplay between helicity and extended hopping ranges, analyzing how they jointly control the energy-resolved and dc pumped currents under time-periodic end potentials. For LRH, the pumped dc current exhibits pronounced plateau-like regions as a function of chemical potential when energy levels are sparsely spaced -- consistent with adiabatic transport -- whereas SRH yields more parameter-sensitive currents without clear plateaus. The plateau stability is controlled by the drive frequency: at higher frequencies, Floquet side-band mixing destroys the plateaus, leading to oscillatory currents. The phase dependence remains nearly sinusoidal, and the current vanishes at zero phase lag, confirming the necessity of out-of-phase potentials. Crucially, in helical systems, the decay exponent $(\ell_c)$ acts as an effective structural parameter that can tune both the magnitude and sign of the pumped current, offering a geometric knob for controlling quantum pumping. Our findings not only fill a gap in the understanding of spectral and pumped currents in helical systems with extended hopping but also provide tools that can be applied to analyze similar phenomena in other chiral or quasi-one-dimensional systems.
summary: The paper theoretically investigates quantum charge pumping in helical nanostructures by comparing short-range versus long-range electron hopping models. Using the Keldysh NEGF formalism, the authors find that long-range hopping facilitates robust, plateau-like DC currents characteristic of adiabatic pumping. Crucially, they demonstrate that the structural decay exponent ($\ell_c$) provides a geometric control mechanism to tune this quantum current.
main_problem: The study investigates quantum charge pumping mechanisms in single-stranded helical structures by comparing the effects of short-range versus long-range electron hopping.
main_result: Long-range hopping (LRH) leads to pronounced plateau-like regions in the pumped DC current under adiabatic conditions, while the decay exponent ($\ell_c$) acts as a structural knob to tune this current. Short-range hopping (SRH) yields less robust and more parameter-sensitive currents.
method: The Keldysh non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) approach is employed to calculate time-dependent transport properties, utilizing the Floquet representation for periodically driven systems.
model_or_system: The system is a single-stranded helical molecule modeled by a tight-binding Hamiltonian incorporating both short-range and long-range hopping terms. The structure is subjected to time-periodic potentials applied at its ends.
key_observables: Spectral current, pumped DC current ($J_{dc}$), dependence of $J_{dc}$ on chemical potential ($\mu$), phase lag ($\delta$), driving frequency ($\Omega$), and amplitude ($V_p$).
important_parameters: Hopping range (SRH vs. LRH), decay exponent ($\ell_c$), drive frequency ($\Omega$), and phase difference ($\delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis compares adiabatic transport regimes (low $\Omega$) with high-frequency, non-adiabatic dynamics; the use of NEGF formalism is central to the calculation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $J_{dc}$ vs. $\mu$ showing plateaus for LRH in the adiabatic limit; they also show the dependence on phase lag ($\delta$), driving amplitude ($V_p$), and spectral function comparisons across different hopping regimes and frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the theoretical predictions of quantum charge pumping using NEGF, first establishing the model Hamiltonian for helical systems, then analyzing the distinct transport signatures arising from LRH versus SRH under varying driving conditions ($\Omega$, $\delta$).
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding how geometric structure (chirality/helicity) and electronic coupling range influence non-equilibrium quantum transport phenomena in low-dimensional molecular conductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (Color online). Schematic illustration of a right- handed single-stranded helical molecule sandwiched between two reservoirs kept at the same chemical potential, as a pump- ing device. R represents the radius, ∆ϕ is the twisting angle, and ∆h denotes the stacking distance between the neighbor- ing lattice sites.
FIG. 3: (Color online). The pumped dc current as a function of normalized chemical potential (µ) for LRH and SRH con- figurations, with Vp = 0.5, Ω= 0.01, and δ = π/2.
FIG. 2: (Color online). The current spectral function versus the electron energy for (a) LRH and (b) SRH configurations with Vp = 0.5 and Ω= 0.01.
FIG. 4: (Color online). The pumped dc current as a function of the phase lag (δ) for LRH and SRH configurations with µ = 0.5, Vp = 0.5, and Ω= 0.01.
FIG. 6: (Color online). The current spectral function versus the energy (ω) for (a) LRH and (b) SRH configurations with Vp = 0.5, Ω= 0.3, and δ = π/2.
